data.title: DHS launches 'Operation Catch of the Day' enforcement action in Maine
data.description:
The Department of Homeland Security’s (DHS) newest immigration crackdown, “Operation Catch of the Day,” is underway in Maine. 

“We have launched Operation Catch of the Day to target the worst of the worst criminal illegal aliens in the state,” DHS Assistant Secretary Tricia McLaughlin said in a Wednesday statement. “On the first day of operations, we arrested illegal aliens charged and convicted of aggravated assault, false imprisonment, and endangering the welfare of a child.”

Additionally, McLaughlin criticized the state’s Democratic governor, saying Janet Mills and fellow Maine officials “have made it abundantly clear that they would rather stand with criminal illegal aliens than protect law-abiding American citizens.”

In a video posted on the social platform X prior to DHS’s announcement, Mills addressed speculation that the department was planning to deploy federal authorities to the state. The governor said in the Jan. 14 clip that she has been coordinating with local law enforcement and the state’s attorney general’s office to prepare a response to the operation.

 “If any operations take place, our goal — as always — will be to protect the safety and the rights of the people of Maine,” Mills said. 

Mills went on to criticize Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers’ tactics in previous operations, saying Maine’s law enforcement is “held to high professional standards.”

“I’ll tell you this, they don’t wear a mask to shield their identities, and they don’t arrest people in order to fill a quota,” Mills said. “To the federal government, I say this: If your plan is to come here to be provocative and to undermine the civil rights of Maine residents, do not be confused. Those tactics are not welcome here.”

Following the operation’s announcement, Maine Sen. Susan Collins (R) affirmed DHS’s authority to arrest undocumented immigrants in the state, but she cautioned that these operations should not impact immigrants who are in the country legally.

“Immigrants have long been an important part of our state, and they continue to make valuable contributions to our communities,” Collins said in a Wednesday statement. “People who are in this country legally should not be targets of ICE investigations. They should be able to continue to go to work, school, and other public places and carry on with their normal lives.”

Additionally, the senator urged ICE to provide their officers with body cameras and “de-escalation training.”

“At this time of heightened tensions, these steps could help improve trust, accountability, and safety.”

Collins noted that DHS’s funding bill, which is currently under consideration in Congress, allocates money for these measures. 

The Maine operation follows DHS’s weeks-long advances in Minnesota, which resulted in clashes between residents and federal officers after the fatal shooting of Renee Good by an ICE officer. 

Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) said in a statement this week that families are “scared” and children “are afraid to go to school” because of federal officers’ actions.

The Justice Department has since launched an investigation into five of the state’s officials, including Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D). Walz called the investigation a “partisan distraction” and said his state “will not be drawn into political theater.

Mills earlier this month expressed her support of Mainers’ rights to protest, but she urged them to do so peacefully and “to meet any hostility with reserve and resolve.”

Additionally, the governor echoed the words of Minnesotan officials, who have repeatedly lambasted the Trump administration for using tactics they allege are meant to unnerve their constituents and state officials. 

“Maine will not be intimidated,” Mills said. “We will not betray the values that make us who we are.”

data.title: Do ICE officers really need to wear face masks?
data.description:
The Trump administration has arrested and deported a substantial number of noncriminal aliens who are not a threat to safety or national security. This controversial policy has resulted in an extraordinary amount of hostility, which has led to an increase in a practice known as “doxing.” 

The Department of Homeland Security defines “doxing” as “gathering an individual’s personally identifiable information dand releasing it publicly for malicious purposes, such as public humiliation, stalking, identity theft, or targeting for harassment.” Personally identifiable information may include an individual’s “full name, contact information, home address, family members, workplace details, financial information, and social security number.” 

But doxing isn’t just an invasion of privacy, according to Jimmy Valenzuela, special agent in charge at the ICE Office of Professional Responsibility. “It is a deliberate act of intimidation that undermines the safety and security of ICE employees who are dedicated to upholding the law and protecting our nation.” 

In an Oct. 30 press release, DHS Assistant Secretary Tricia McLaughlin says that ICE officers are facing an 8,000 percent increase in death threats, and that their families have been doxed and threatened.

In one instance, a federal grand jury issued an indictment charging three women with following an ICE officer home and then posting his home address on Instagram.  

In another, an ICE officer’s spouse in Texas received a call saying, “I don’t know how you let your husband work for ICE, and you sleep at night. F–k you, f–k your family. I hope your kids get deported by accident. How do you sleep? F–k you. Did you hear what happened to the Nazis after World War II? Because it’s what’s going to happen to your family.”    

In Massachusetts, a woman allegedly coordinated a group on the Tinder dating app to dupe ICE officers into dates to obtain their personal information and then broadcast it online. 

Federal law authorizes fines and imprisonment for up to five years for doxxing if it is done “with the intent to threaten, intimidate, or incite the commission of a crime of violence” against the person being doxed or members of his or her family. ICE officers need to be able to enforce immigration laws without fear of being subjected to such threats. McLaughlin has said that those found to be doxing ICE officers “will be prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law.”  

The simplest way to stop doxing would be to limit interior enforcement to criminal aliens. In fact, President Trump has pledged to start his enforcement program by deporting “the worst of the worst.” He said he would remove millions of these criminal aliens with the largest deportation program in American history. But this doesn’t mean that he intended to shield deportable non-criminal aliens until he removed all of the criminal aliens.

In fact, presidents are prohibited from categorically shielding non-criminal aliens from deportation. The oath of office for presidents requires them to swear they will “preserve, protect and defend the Constitution of the United States.” According to Supreme Court Justice Samuel Alito, such a categorical restriction on the enforcement of our immigration laws would violate a president’s duty under Article II, Section 3 of the U.S. Constitution “to take Care that the Laws be faithfully executed.” 

This doesn’t mean that a president can’t prioritize his immigration enforcement measures, of course. It just means that “he must act in good faith” to meet his “take care” duty. But it would go beyond prioritization if ICE officers were to be restricted to enforcing deportation provisions in the Immigration and Nationality Act that pertain to criminal aliens.   

Back to doxing: Is it necessary that ICE officers wear masks? 

Border czar Tom Homan has admitted that he doesn’t “like the look of officers wearing masks.” But, he added, if doxing has made masks necessary to protect them and their families, “it has to be done.”  

The strongest objection to face masks is that it reduces accountability. This is a legitimate concern. But there are ways to identify ICE officers who are wearing masks. For instance, ICE headquarters could make a list of officers who engage in enforcement activities, assign a number to each officer, and then put the numbers on the uniforms they wear when they are conducting enforcement activities. 

This would make it possible for the Office of Inspector General, federal courts and congressional oversight committees to identify officers who are the subjects of complaints for allegedly engaging in improper conduct, merely by looking up their numbers.   

Nolan Rappaport was detailed to the House Judiciary Committee as an Executive Branch Immigration Law Expert for three years. He subsequently served as an immigration counsel for the Subcommittee on Immigration, Border Security and Claims for four years. Prior to working on the Judiciary Committee, he wrote decisions for the Board of Immigration Appeals for 20 years.

data.title: Will Trump's plan to limit institutional investors lower home prices?
data.description:
(NewsNation) — President Trump wants to make it harder for large institutional investors to buy single-family homes, but the effort may not deliver the affordability relief many are hoping for.

Trump signed an executive order Tuesday directing federal agencies to issue guidance aimed at stopping Wall Street from competing with Main Street for homes. But key details — including how “large institutional investor” and “single-family home” will be defined — may not be known for several weeks.

The president argued in the order that Wall Street firms have made it harder for young families to compete for starter homes and said large investors have been “treating America’s neighborhoods like a trading floor.”

“Homes are built for people, not for corporations — and America will not become a nation of renters,” Trump said Wednesday at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

Efforts to rein in institutional investors have typically been associated with Democrats, who have pushed to curb corporate homebuyers in recent years.

While the idea may have political appeal, data suggests both parties could be overstating how much institutional investors shape the national housing market — particularly when it comes to affordability and home prices.

Will Trump’s plan make homes more affordable?

Key details are still being worked out, but housing experts are skeptical limiting institutional investors would meaningfully improve affordability for everyday Americans.

“Large-scale institutional investors make up a very small percentage of homeownership in the country, and investors have mostly acted as net home sellers rather than buyers in the recent past,” Realtor.com senior economist Joel Berner said in an email.

Large investors who own 50 or more properties accounted for less than 2 percent of all single-family home purchases last year, according to Realtor.com. That share peaked in 2022 at 3.5 percent.

A separate report from the center-right American Enterprise Institute also found that institutional investors remain “relatively minor players” in the single-family housing market. As of November, institutional investors — defined as those owning 100-plus rental homes — owned only 1 percent of the nation’s single-family housing stock, according to AEI’s analysis of Parcl Labs data.

In some cities, particularly in Sun Belt states, research suggests that institutional investors make up a larger portion of the single-family rental market than the national average, meaning Trump’s effort could be felt differently depending on where you live.

But even if limiting institutional investors modestly reduces competition in those markets, Redfin chief economist Daryl Fairweather argues it doesn’t solve the underlying issue.

“If policymakers want to meaningfully lower housing costs, the focus needs to remain on building more homes and reforming barriers to new supply,” Fairweather said in a statement.

One safeguard for supply is a carve-out for “build-to-rent” activity, where large institutional investors partner with homebuilders to create entire neighborhoods. That exception, which Trump included in the order, should prevent major disruptions to new single-family construction, Berner said.

Are institutional investors driving up home prices?

Home prices skyrocketed during the pandemic, but research suggests institutional investors aren’t the main reason why.

A 2022 study by Freddie Mac identified four primary drivers behind the pandemic-era spike in home prices:

  • Record-low mortgage rates in 2020 and 2021, which boosted demand
  • Limited supply due to years of underbuilding
  • A wave of first-time home buyers driven by age demographics
  • Increased migration from high-cost cities to areas that already had a housing shortage

“Although investors are certainly contributing to the affordable housing shortage, they are not the driver of the tight supply of homes available for purchase,” Freddie Mac concluded.

Still, local differences exist.

A 2024 analysis by the Government Accountability Office found that large institutions owned 25 percent of single-family rental homes in Atlanta, 21 percent in Jacksonville, Fla., and 18 percent in Charlotte, N.C.

The GAO report noted that today’s institutional investor presence emerged out of the Great Recession, when firms began bulk-purchasing foreclosed homes and converting them into rentals.

Studies that the nonpartisan agency reviewed found no investor owned 1,000 or more single-family rental homes as of late 2011. But by the end of 2022, the five largest investors owned about 300,000 homes — nearly 2 percent of all single-family rental homes nationally.

The GAO ultimately concluded that while institutional investors “may have contributed” to price increases following the financial crisis, their effect on homeownership opportunities was “unclear.”

There’s another tension to consider: Affordability has recently been improving in some of the same Sun Belt metros where institutional investor activity is higher than the national average.

In those markets, inventory has risen sharply due to broader market forces, said Jake Krimmel, senior economist at Realtor.com. Some of those cities have even seen home prices fall, in part, thanks to a construction boom.

“In the supply-constrained Northeast, corporate investor activity is minimal, so the policy would have little to no impact on inventory,” Krimmel said.

He added, “Policies that expand housing supply in the long run will do more to improve affordability than restricting a small and shrinking class of buyers.”

data.title: Immigration officers assert sweeping power to enter homes without judge's warrant: Memo
data.description:
Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers are asserting broad authority to enter people’s homes without first obtaining a warrant from a judge, according to an internal memo.

The memo signed by the acting ICE director Todd Lyons, and obtained by The Associated Press, authorizes immigration officers to forcibly enter someone’s home and arrest immigrants using just a signed administrative warrant, known as an I-205, if they have a final order of removal issued by a judge.  

Typically, administrative warrants authorize immigration arrests but do not permit officers to forcibly enter private homes without consent. Traditionally, only warrants signed by judges carry that authority. But the memo says the department has come to a different conclusion, the AP reported.

“Although the U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) has not historically relied on administrative warrants alone to arrest aliens subject to final orders of removal in their place of residence, the DHS Office of the General Counsel has recently determined that the U.S. Constitution, the Immigration and Nationality Act, and the immigration regulations do not prohibit relying on administrative warrants for this purpose,” the memo reads. 

It also instructs officers first to knock on the door and identify themselves and the reason for their visit. If people inside are then given a “reasonable chance to act lawfully,” but do not open the door, the memo says the officers can enter with force.  

“Should the alien refuse admittance, ICE officers and agents should use only a necessary and reasonable amount of force to enter the alien’s residence, following proper notification of the officer or agent’s authority and intent to enter,” the document reads.

The memo was issued last May and has only been shared with “select DHS officials,” according to a whistleblower disclosure obtained by the AP.

But the contents of the directive have been used to train ICE officers who have been deployed to cities during the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown, according to the document, which claims immigration enforcement officers undergoing training are instructed to follow the memo, even though it contradicts the agency’s written training materials. 

The news wire obtained the whistleblower disclosure from an unnamed member of Congress and said it verified the authenticity of the accounts in the complaint. 

The nonprofit legal organization, Whistleblower Aid, filed a complaint, saying it represents two government officials “disclosing a secretive — and seemingly unconstitutional — policy directive.” 

Homeland Security Assistant Secretary Tricia McLaughlin stood by the reasoning provided in the internal memo, saying in a statement to The Hill, “Every illegal alien who DHS serves administrative warrants/I-205s have had full due process and a final order of removal from an immigration judge.” 

“The officers issuing these administrative warrants also have found probable cause. For decades, the Supreme Court and Congress have recognized the propriety of administrative warrants in cases of immigration enforcement,” she added.

The whistleblower disclosure, dated earlier this month, comes amid escalating tensions in cities across the country over the Trump administration’s aggressive approach to immigration enforcement. 

In Minnesota, protests have at times turned violent, as demonstrators have chucked rocks and fireworks at law enforcement, who have responded with tear gas and force. Two officer-involved shootings, including one that was fatal, have also further inflamed tensions. 

The Associated Press contributed.

data.title: Cuban immigrant in ICE custody died of homicide: Autopsy
data.description:
A Cuban immigrant’s death in the custody of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) in Texas has been ruled a homicide, according to The Associated Press.

ICE said in a press release earlier this month that on Jan. 3, a Cuban migrant named Geraldo Lunas Campos had died while in custody. The agency said Lunas Campos, who was at a detention facility in El Paso, Texas, had been “disruptive” and “placed in segregation” on the day he died. 

The agency claimed that he was later seen “in distress,” with medical staff intervening and attempting to save his life, but was eventually found to be dead.

However, an autopsy report on Wednesday that concluded Lunas Campos’s death had been a homicide, according to the AP.  According to the report from the El Paso County Medical Examiner’s Office, knee and chest abrasions were discovered on Lunas Campos’s body, indications of a struggle, the outlet reported. 

The medical examiner’s office also said Lunas Campos’s neck had hemorrhages, the AP reported, with the deputy medical examiner finding Lunas Campos to have died from asphyxia as a result of neck and torso compression.

ICE has claimed that Lunas Campos had multiple prior convictions, including criminal possession of a weapon, larceny and sexual contact with a child.

Throughout his first year of his second term, President Trump’s administration has faced considerable criticism for its immigration agenda. However, that criticism has flared in the wake of an ICE officer fatally shooting a woman in Minneapolis, with Democrats widely condemning the incident and the city’s mayor pushing for the agency to leave.

The criticism has also covered the detention of migrants held throughout the country.

The Hill has reached out to ICE for comment.

data.title: Paramilitary forces have no place on American streets
data.description:
Democratic societies need soldiers and police. When they combine the two into a single body, tragedies like the Minnesota shooting are inevitable.

Forty years of studying internal security operations around the world have taught me that units empowered as police but armed like soldiers will lack the discipline of either. The behavior of federal agents conducting the administration’s immigration crackdown clearly demonstrates the danger they pose.

Soldiers operate under “rules of engagement,” guidelines defining the circumstances in which they can use lethal force. In wartime, those guidelines are quite permissive, but even then there are still restrictions. The “Law of War,” a set of agreed upon international principles accepted by most militaries, forbids (for example) the intentional killing of non-combatants and the abuse of prisoners.

Armed forces around the world abide by these laws — not merely on moral grounds, but to make sure their own troops and civilians receive fair treatment. The outrage over the killing of two men clinging to a burning boat by U.S. forces in September illustrates the wide regard in which these principles are held. 

Because of their professional ethos, soldiers in democratic societies assiduously avoid internal security operations. When troops have been deployed in that role — which occurred in Northern Ireland during the Troubles (1969 to 1998) — problems invariably arise. The Jan. 30, 1972 “Bloody Sunday” massacre of demonstrators in Londonderry by members of the Parachute Regiment still haunts the British Army.

Police operate under far more restrictive rules of engagement than soldiers. According to Department of Justice guidelines, officers may only use deadly force when “the officer has a reasonable belief that the subject of such force poses an imminent danger of death or serious physical injury to the officer or to another person.” The rules explicitly forbid use of lethal force “solely to prevent the escape of a fleeing suspect.” 

Whereas soldiers and police have a clear understanding of their rules of engagement, the same cannot be said of hybrid paramilitary forces. The behavior of the U.S. Customs and Border Protection, which includes the Border Patrol and Immigration and Customs Enforcement illustrates the problem.

Even before the Trump administration’s massive roundup of illegal immigrants, the Border Patrol had been accused of using excessive force. Since 2010, the force has killed 362 people. Equipped with military grade weapons and body armor, members of this paramilitary force often behave like soldiers, even though they are technically police. 

Before Trump took office last January, the Border Patrol confined its operations primarily to the southern border. Now it operates in U.S. cities alongside ICE agents, who are also heavily armed.

In addition to deploying Customs and Border Protection agents more extensively, Trump has given them freer rein. He has lifted restrictions on immigration raids in “sensitive” areas such as schools, churches and hospitals. In September, the Supreme Court upheld the power of Customs and Border Patrol and ICE to apprehend people based on their language, location and appearance — what many experts call “racial profiling.”

Although operating under law enforcement rules of engagement, agents show up in body armor and carrying assault weapons and behave more like soldiers than police. 

The rapid expansion of ICE and its recruitment methods have led to concerns over vetting and training. Cash incentives and an appeal to rid the country of undocumented immigrants, described in dehumanizing terms, may be drawing recruits with a vigilante mentality. Pressure to deploy more agents as fast as possible makes it difficult to train them properly. 

To make matters worse, under Secretary Kristi Noem, the Department of Homeland Security has created a permissive institutional culture in which its agencies feel empowered to use violence with little fear of accountability. Their officers and agents wear masks and do not show identification or warrants. 

Noem’s response to the Minneapolis shooting illustrates her permissive attitude. Before an investigation had even occurred, she defended the shooting of Renee Good by ICE agent Jonathan Ross, portraying Good as a “domestic terrorist.” The FBI has taken over the investigation and is excluding Minnesota officials, even though murder is a state crime over which local authorities have jurisdiction. 

Deploying paramilitary forces with police powers, a military mentality and an aggressive mindset without proper training and oversight leads to the abuses of power witnessed in Minneapolis. Since the death of Good, two more people have been shot (not fatally) by a federal agent in Portland, Oregon. More tragedies are bound to occur. 

As a military historian who focuses on violent extremism, I have examined internal conflicts throughout history and across the world to learn how distinct types of government respond to threats. Democracies rely on disciplined police. They rarely deploy troops inside their countries but, when they do, employ them in an extremely limited role, such as riot-control.

Dictatorships, on the other hand, rely heavily on paramilitary forces that are answerable only to the regime. 

We are heading down a dark and dangerous road. 

Tom Mockaitis is a professor of history at DePaul University and the author of “Violent Extremists: Understanding the Domestic and International Terrorist Threat .”

data.title: Even Trump can't escape the presidential foreign-policy trap
data.description:
More often than not, Americans elect presidents based on domestic issues. But presidents are inevitably dragged into foreign policy issues and problems. President Trump is no exception.

Fittingly for Trump, his situation is more extreme than most. Elected under the banner of “America First,” Trump has not so much been dragged into foreign affairs as he has enthusiastically embraced it. And that could prove dangerous. 

The American public does tend to support the president in national security crises. But the reality is that, absent the perception of a real national security threat, Americans tune out international affairs. Americans are insular. When asked to rank their top issue, foreign policy consistently lags issues like inflation, the economy  and health care — usually barely topping 1 percent.

And this makes sense. The U.S. is less connected to outside countries than any other developed economy. As a percentage of GDP, its trade (imports and exports) is at a lower percentage than for any other developed economy, and most undeveloped ones. Sourced from World Bank data, imports were just 15 percent of GDP, nearly 10 points less than Japan. And exports were under 12 percent — 21 points below France, which is lowest in the European Union. 

This disconnect gives presidents a lot of leeway in foreign policy. As long as the economy is doing well, international affairs are not a problem. And when the economy goes bad — see Jimmy Carter and George H.W. Bush — no amount of international success helps.

Alternatively, presidents whose foreign adventures turn into a quagmire can see their presidencies crippled (see Lyndon Johnson and George W. Bush). 

Thus, the challenge for Trump is not just to make his foreign policy work, but — much more importantly — to make hiw domestic policy succeed. 

RealClearPolitics averages overall approval and by issue. At the time of writing this piece, Trump was net negative 15 points in overall approval, and down 7 points in the Trump-favorable Rasmussen poll. His foreign policy approval was down to a similar level. But polling on inflation is especially bad — down 23 points. And he is down 15 points on the economy in general. 

On the two domestic issues where Trump has been most focused, his numbers are much better. Despite the controversy in Minnesota, Trump is only down 4 percent on immigration on average, and just 1.3 percent on crime. Both Morning Consult and RMG Research have Trump net positive in their most recent polls on immigration. 

Looking at the most recent YouGov benchmark (a somewhat Trump-unfavorable survey), the poll tells the same story. Trump’s personal approval is negative 15 points, but he performs 8 points better on crime and 5 points better on immigration. On inflation, he’s12 points worse.  

The problem Trump has is that his best issues are not the top concern of the overall public and independents (i.e., swing voters). Immigration was the top issue of 12 percent of the public and just 6 percent of independents. Crime is a lowly 2 percent (4 percent for independents). Meanwhile, inflation was at 22 percent and 25 percent, respectively. 

The more Trump-favorable Wall Street Journal poll has the president’s favorability at negative 7 points. But again, his domestic policy numbers are a problem. He performs 3 points worse on the economy, 5 points worse on health care and 10 points worse on inflation. Confirming other polls, Trump is 3 points better on immigration and 6 points net positive on “border security.” 

The curious thing is that Trump is getting a bad rap on inflation. The Economist, a strongly anti-Trump publication, did a deep dive into the numbers and found that inflation has cooled significantly under Trump. Even more importantly, real incomes (income adjusted for inflation) are rising under Trump — and rising more for lower-income Americans. 

The problem for Trump is not entirely of his own making. After years of tame inflation, the shock under former President Joe Biden was severe. And the bigger the shock to the public, the longer it takes to get used to the new normal.

Trump has not ignored the issue; he has proposed banning private equity from owning homes, presumably to force more supply into the market. He has worked to get deals for cheaper drugs and put caps on credit card interest rates. 

But the problem for Trump is twofold.  

First, there is not a coherent policy he is promoting. It’s a lot of opportunism. Cutting housing costs requires a broad set of policy moves, as does reining in health care costs. Trump needs to announce a big set of initiatives in each area, not drip out one-off deals. 

The second problem is one of emphasis. Each of Trump’s recent shots at inflation have been swamped by his foreign policy moves. His proposal to cap credit card interest rates has been drowned by talk of acquiring Greenland, something never even broached in his 2024 campaign. And support is weak, with just 29 percent in favor (YouGov poll), including just 58 percent of Republicans — a very low number for Trump. 

For nearly four years, the American public has identified inflation as the No.1 issue, and by overwhelming numbers. Joe Biden and Kamala Harris ignored it. If Trump continues to allow himself to be distracted away from tackling inflation, he is likely to pay a big price. 

Keith Naughton, a longtime Republican political consultant, is co-founder of Silent Majority Strategies, a public and regulatory affairs consulting firm, and a former Republican political campaign consultant in Pennsylvania.

data.title: Live updates: Jack Smith testifies in House; major allies absent at Trump's Board of Peace ceremony
data.description:
Former special counsel Jack Smith is testifying on Capitol Hill Thursday morning, in his first public testimony about his investigations into President Trump. 

Smith, per his opening remarks, will stand by his decision to probe Trump over efforts to overturn the 2020 election and his mishandling of classified documents

“No one should be above the law in our country, and the law required that [Trump] be held to account. So that is what I did,” Smith will say in his remarks. 

Trump is on his way back to the U.S. from Davos, Switzerland, where he met with world leaders and unveiled his new Board of Peace in Gaza at the annual World Economic Forum. 

Earlier Thursday, the president participated in a signing ceremony for his peace board initiative alongside a handful of world leaders. No major European allies attended the Thursday signing ceremony, however, as tensions flare with NATO amid Trump’s bid to acquire Greenland

After he delivered remarks Wednesday, Trump announced that allies reached a “framework” of a deal on his quest to acquire Greenland. The president also said he would no longer impose tariffs on Denmark and other NATO countries, walking back threats to forcefully take over the island nation.

Trump also huddled with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky Thursday morning in closed door meeting, amid his efforts to bring about an end to Russia’s nearly four-year-war in Ukraine.

Vice President Vance, meanwhile, will travel to Toledo, Ohio, and then Minneapolis, where protests have flared over federal immigration enforcement efforts across the state.

Follow live below.

data.title: Trump predicts 'Democrat shutdown' as funding deadline looms
data.description:
President Trump predicted in an interview on Thursday that the U.S. was likely headed toward another shutdown before the funding deadline at the end of January.

“I think we have a problem, because I think we’re going to probably end up in another Democrat shutdown,” Trump told host Maria Bartiromo on Fox Business’s “Mornings with Maria.”

“The shutdown cost us a lot, and I think they’ll probably do it again, that’s my feeling. We’ll see what happens,” he continued. 

Lawmakers in the House and the Senate have until Jan. 30 to avoid a government shutdown. So far, they have passed six out of the 12 appropriations bills needed to fund the government. Last week the House passed an additional two-bill package known as a minibus that would fund the State Department and the Treasury Department.  Senators will take that minibus up after they come back from their recess next week.

So far, there appears to be little appetite among Democrats for another shutdown.

However, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) appropriations bill has become a significant point of contention between both parties.

Democrats in the House and the Senate have called for an overhaul of Immigration Customs Enforcement after an Immigration and Customs Enforcement officer fatally shot an unarmed woman in Minneapolis. Democrats say they will oppose the DHS bill unless it includes tougher oversight and conduct guidelines for ICE officers. 

The last government shutdown began in the beginning of October and went on for 43 days. That shutdown was over extending Affordable Care Act, or Obamacare subsidies.

data.title: 'Sinners' sets Oscars record with 16 nominations
data.description:
Ryan Coogler’s blues-steeped vampire epic “Sinners” led all films with 16 nominations to the 98th Academy Awards on Thursday, setting a record for the most in Oscar history.

Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences voters showered “Sinners” with more nominations than they had ever bestowed before, breaking the 14-nomination mark set by “All About Eve,” “Titanic” and “La La Land.” Along with best picture, Coogler was nominated for best director and best screenplay, and double-duty star Michael B. Jordan was rewarded with his first Oscar nomination, for best actor.

Paul Thomas Anderson’s father-daughter revolutionary saga “One Battle After Another,” the favorite coming into nominations, trailed in second with 13 nominations of its own. Four of its actors — Leonardo DiCaprio, Teyana Taylor, Benicio del Toro and Sean Penn — were nominated, though newcomer Chase Infiniti was left out in best actress.

In those two top nominees, the film academy put its full force behind a pair of visceral and bracingly original American epics that each connected with a fraught national moment. Coogler’s Jim Crow-era film — the rare horror movie to win the academy’s favor — conjures a mythical allegory of Black life. In “One Battle After Another,” a dormant spirit of rebellion is revived in an out-of-control police state.

Both are also Warner Bros. titles. In the midst of a contentious sale to Netflix, the 102-year-old studio had its best Oscar nominations morning ever. As the fate of Warner Bros., which Netflix is buying for $72 billion, hangs in the balance amid a challenge from Paramount Skydance, Hollywood is bracing for potentially the largest realignment in the film industry’s history.

The 10 films nominated for best picture are “Bugonia,” “F1,” “Frankenstein,” “Hamnet,” “Marty Supreme,” “One Battle After Another,” “The Secret Agent,” “Sentimental Value,” “Sinners” and “Train Dreams.”

Guillermo del Toro’s “Frankenstein,” Josh Safdie’s “Marty Supreme” and Joachim Trier’s “Sentimental Value” all scored nine nominations.

The nine for “Marty Supreme” included a third best actor nod for 30-year-old Timothée Chalamet, the favorite in the category he narrowly missed winning last year for “A Complete Unknown.” With Jordan and Chalamet, the nominees are Leonardo DiCaprio for “One Battle After Another,” Ethan Hawke for “Blue Moon” and Wagner Moura for “The Secret Agent.”

Nominated for best actress was the category favorite, Jessie Buckley (“Hamnet”), along with Rose Byrne (“If I Had Legs I’d Kick You”), Kate Hudson (“Song Sung Blue”), Renate Reinsve (“Sentimental Value”) and two-time winner Emma Stone, who landed her sixth nomination, for “Bugonia.”

The year’s most-watched movie, with more than half a billion views on Netflix, “KPop Demon Hunters,” scored nominations for both best song (“Golden”) and best animated feature. Sony Pictures developed and produced the film, but, after selling it to Netflix, watched it become a worldwide sensation.

Blockbusters otherwise had a difficult morning. Universal Pictures’ “Wicked: For Good” was shut out entirely. While “Avatar: Fire and Ash” notched nominations for costume design and visual effects, it became the first “Avatar” film not nominated for best picture. The biggest box-office hit nominated for Hollywood’s top award instead was “F1,” an Apple production. The streamer partnered with Warner Bros. to distribute the racing drama.

The first category read by presenters Danielle Brooks and Lewis Pullman was supporting actress. The nominees are Elle Fanning and Inga Ibsdotter LilIeaas for “Sentimental Value,” Amy Madigan for “Weapons,” Wunmi Mosaku for “Sinners” and Teyana Taylor for “One Battle After Another.”

For supporting actor, the nominees are Jacob Elordi for “Frankenstein,” Sean Penn for “One Battle After Another,” Stellan Skarsgård for “Sentimental Value,” Benicio del Toro for “One Battle After Another” and Delroy Lindo for “Sinners.”

This year, the Oscars are introducing a new category for casting. That new honor helped “Sinners” and “One Battle After Another” pad their already impressive stats. Along with those two films, the nominees are “Hamnet,” “Marty Supreme” and “The Secret Agent.”

The 98th Academy Awards will take place on March 15 at the Dolby Theatre in Los Angeles and will be televised live on ABC and Hulu. YouTube’s new deal to exclusively air won’t take effect until 2029. This year, Conan O’Brien will return as host.
